summary:
The suppression of the East African slave trade, centered on the island sultanate of Zanzibar, was one of the major humanitarian and political projects of British policy in the Indian Ocean world across the nineteenth century. Zanzibar had been the hub of the East African slave trade since the Omani Sultanate established its capital there in 1840, exporting hundreds of thousands of enslaved Africans to the Arabian Peninsula, Persia, India, and to the clove plantations of Zanzibar itself over the following decades.

British diplomatic pressure produced a series of treaties restricting and eventually prohibiting the trade. The Moresby Treaty of 1822 began restricting trade to certain zones; the Hamerton Treaty of 1845 prohibited the export of enslaved people outside the Sultan's African dominions; and the 1873 treaty, negotiated under threat of naval blockade by Sir Bartle Frere with Sultan Barghash, formally closed Zanzibar's slave market and prohibited all seaborne trading. David Livingstone's explorations and his accounts of the interior slave trade had intensified British public pressure for suppression, particularly after his death in 1873 transformed him into a martyred symbol of the abolitionist cause.

Suppression in practice was difficult and slow. The Royal Navy's antislavery patrol in the Indian Ocean, the East Indies Squadron, intercepted dhows and freed their captives, but the overland trade and domestic slavery on Zanzibar and the mainland continued. The establishment of formal British and German colonial rule over East Africa in the late 1880s provided administrative machinery to enforce abolition more thoroughly, though slavery as a legal institution was not formally abolished on Zanzibar until 1897, and remnants persisted longer. The ending of the East African trade was deeply intertwined with the broader European partition of the continent.
